# `jtframe_cache` Direct Tests

This directory contains the direct simunit tests for `jtframe_cache`.
Each test instantiates the cache against `jtframe_burst_sdram` and the
`mt48lc16m16a2` SDRAM model, preloads deterministic data into SDRAM, and then
checks the cache through its consumer-side interface.

These tests are intentionally focused on the cache by itself. They do not cover
the cache mux; that lives under `modules/jtframe/ver/sdram/cache_mux`.

## Common Setup

Most tests in this directory share a few traits:

- they run the real burst controller path, not a mocked memory handshake
- all current tests use `BLKSIZE=1024`
- they count `ext_ack` pulses to distinguish cache hits from cache misses
- they inject periodic refresh requests; `stress/` does so at a 64 us interval
- they operate on bank 0 only

The tests are mostly deterministic functional checks. The `stress/` case is the
long-form exception: it writes and reads back a generated 512 KiB payload
through the cache while periodic refresh requests are active.

## What The Tests Cover

### `read`

Folder: `read/`

This test runs three cache instances:

- `DW=8`
- `DW=16`
- `DW=32`, `ENDIAN=0`

Coverage:

- first access to a cache line is a miss and opens exactly one SDRAM burst
- repeated accesses inside the same line are hits and do not open new bursts
- first and last units inside a line return the expected data
- crossing from one line into the next causes a new miss
- four different lines that map to the same set can coexist before the first
  one is re-read as a hit
- 32-bit little-endian read assembly is checked against the backing SDRAM words

This is the main read-only sanity test for line fill and hit behavior.

### `rw`

Folder: `rw/`

This test also runs three cache instances:

- `DW=8`
- `DW=16`
- `DW=32`, `ENDIAN=0`

Coverage:

- write miss performs a refill before the write completes
- write hit completes from cache without opening a new burst
- writes do not update SDRAM immediately; data stays dirty in cache until
  eviction
- partial writes through `wdsn` are checked for `DW=16` and `DW=32`
- dirty eviction opens two bursts: write-back plus refill
- SDRAM contents after eviction match the software model
- readback after both hit and refill paths returns the expected updated value

This is the main write-back and dirty-eviction test.

### `big_endian`

Folder: `big_endian/`

This test runs one cache instance:

- `DW=32`, `ENDIAN=1`

Coverage:

- big-endian 32-bit read assembly from two 16-bit SDRAM words
- full-width 32-bit writes
- partial writes to the upper and lower 16-bit halves with big-endian lane
  mapping
- dirty eviction and refill with big-endian data preserved across write-back

This is the dedicated endian-ordering test for the 32-bit path.

### `stress`

Folder: `stress/`

This test runs one cache instance:

- `DW=32`, `ENDIAN=1`
- `BLOCKS=32`
- `BLKSIZE=1024`

Coverage:

- a deterministic 512 KiB binary payload is generated by `init.go`
- SDRAM starts blank and is filled only through `jtframe_cache` writes
- the whole file is written sequentially as 32-bit big-endian words
- the write phase fills the cache and then continues under realistic dirty
  replacement traffic
- the whole file is then read back sequentially through the cache
- the underlying SDRAM half-words are checked directly to confirm big-endian
  storage order
- refresh requests are injected every 64 us while traffic is active
- the run uses an 85.909 MHz clock instead of the smaller smoke-test clocking

This is the large end-to-end data-integrity test for the direct cache path.

### `stress64-128`

Folder: `stress64-128/`

This test runs two cache instances sequentially:

- `DW=64`, `ENDIAN=0`
- `DW=128`, `ENDIAN=0`
- `BLOCKS=32`
- `BLKSIZE=1024`

Coverage:

- a deterministic 512 KiB binary payload is generated by `init.go`
- SDRAM starts blank and is filled only through `jtframe_cache` writes
- the whole file is written sequentially and then read back through the cache
- returned 64-bit and 128-bit words match the original byte stream
- the underlying SDRAM half-words are checked directly to confirm little-endian
  storage order
- refresh requests are injected every 64 us while traffic is active

This is the direct wide-data regression for the new `DW=64` and `DW=128`
paths.

## What These Tests Do Not Cover

The direct cache tests leave several behaviors either untested or only
incidentally exercised:

- `jtframe_cache_mux` arbitration and lane routing
- randomized access patterns or pseudo-random address traffic
- more than one outstanding consumer request, or requests arriving while a
  previous transaction is still busy
- simultaneous `rd` and `wr` rising edges and the documented read-priority rule
- illegal consumer behavior such as changing `addr`, `din`, or `wdsn` before
  `ok`
- victim selection policy on dirty write-back paths beyond the observed
  set-associative miss traffic
- exact replacement ordering once a set overflows after all its ways are valid
- explicit checking of `ext_dst`; the cache tests use the normal burst path, but
  they do not assert first-beat marking semantics themselves
- explicit checking of refresh deferral or burst/refresh interaction beyond the
  nominal periodic `rfsh` stimulus
- SDRAM bank selection other than bank 0
- cache parameter sweeps beyond the tested set:
  `BLKSIZE=1024`, `BLOCKS=8` in `read`, `BLOCKS=1` in `rw` and `big_endian`,
  and `BLOCKS=32` in `stress` and `stress64-128`
- `DW=32`, `ENDIAN=1` coverage outside the dedicated `big_endian` and `stress`
  tests
- malformed parameter combinations such as `ENDIAN=1` with `DW!=32`,
  non-power-of-two `BLOCKS`, or `BLKSIZE<16`
- exhaustive offset coverage inside a line; the tests use representative
  addresses near line starts, middles, and ends

## Practical Reading

If you want to know whether a cache regression is already covered, use this
rough map:

- read fills, hit/miss counting, and basic line boundaries: `read/`
- write-back behavior, masking, and eviction to SDRAM: `rw/`
- 32-bit big-endian packing and write masking: `big_endian/`
- long sequential big-endian write/readback with refresh active: `stress/`
- long sequential 64-bit and 128-bit little-endian write/readback with refresh
  active: `stress64-128/`

If a bug depends on mux arbitration, unusual victim choice, or malformed
consumer handshakes, this directory is probably not enough on its own.
